results.title: Apple MLX vs llama.cpp: Which is Really Faster? (4 Runtimes ...
results.content: Unlike llama.cpp, MLX compiles metal kernels lazily. So, there's a certain warm-up period until performance stabilizes. Though, it's just a theory. We'll need more digging. So, what did we get? On the left, you can see filtered cold prompt half context runs. On the right, full context ones. To avoid measuring this warm-up effect, I feel it's fair to take the fastest numbers for comparison. And do that for all the previous run times, too. The pre-fill speed of these three runs are almost as fast as MLX LM. And the right side, we can only compare to llama.cpp because other run times failed. So, let's gather all the results and see the conclusions. Llama.cpp is the baseline. At 49k context, it does 584 tokens per second for pre-fill and 32 tokens for generation. While LM Studio MLX [...] issue again. Prompt caching isn't working for the second function. Ain't good. I added an estimate of prompt processing tokens per second to this CSV file. Reloaded the model and ran the test one more time. The results are consistent. Prefill speed is similar to llama.cpp, but generation faster than MLX LM. Mhm. Ah, I noticed one discrepancy. LM Studio ignored my request not use thinking for these prompts. Though, that shouldn't have made the generation faster. So, will it run with a full source file, or will it crash my Mac like MLX LM? If this doesn't work, we still have the Ollama card, and we really need a break through here. Oh, no. LM Studio didn't crash. That's great. But, it started swapping towards the very end of the prompt prefill, and swapping to disk is very slow. In total, [...] higher for both stages of the inference. I wish it could load my full context though, and the checkpoints would work consistently and wouldn't push my Mac off a cliff. We must try LM Studio. By the way, if you're enjoying this, leave a like and subscribe. It's using the same MLX LM version under the hood, but it never crashes. Let's load the model. Mhm, we're running full precision KV cache today, and let's run the test with half the source file and 49k tokens. Brum brum brum, fast forward and we have the first number. Oh, that's interesting. It completed the prefill in 85 seconds. That's the speed close to llama.cpp, and not MLX LM. The generation speed is very good, though. Even faster than with MLX LM. And we hit the same issue again. Prompt caching isn't working for the second

results.title: Speed Test #2: Llama.CPP vs MLX with Llama-3.3-70B ...
Llama.cpp vs MLX on Apple Mx
results.content:
The Groundy benchmarks make this concrete. On an M1 Max running Qwen3.5–35B at 8.5K context, MLX reported 51 tokens per second in the UI but the effective wall-clock throughput including prefill collapsed to about 3 tokens per second. llama.cpp with `--flash-attn` finished the same response 9 seconds faster (43.4 seconds versus 52.3 seconds). At long contexts, the prefill gap is real.

This is the part of the comparison that resists the clean narrative. MLX has a fused attention kernel, but llama.cpp has Flash Attention proper, and at long contexts that matters. The community is closing the gap (see TurboQuant-MLX, Open-TQ-Metal), but as of today, llama.cpp with `--flash-attn` is faster on long contexts than MLX out of the box.

## Where each one wins [...] Apple Silicon performance for the common case. For 4-bit quantized models in the 7B to 30B range, MLX is typically faster than llama.cpp on the same hardware. The gap widens with the M5 Neural Accelerators, where Apple’s recent benchmarks show 3x speedups over M4 on certain workloads.
 fine-tuning. LoRA, QLoRA, and DoRA are in the box. llama.cpp is inference-only. If you are doing parameter-efficient fine-tuning on a Mac Studio, MLX is the only serious option.
 Swift integration. mlx-swift makes native macOS and iOS apps with local inference straightforward in a way that no llama.cpp wrapper does. For client work where the deliverable is a native macOS application, this is decisive. [...] This is not unfinished. It is a different theory of where complexity should live. llama.cpp says the engine should ship every option as a flag because users should not write code. MLX says the engine should ship a clean numerical core, and complex behavior is a programming task.

Whether that is good or bad depends on who you are. If you are running a production server and want to enable DRY by setting a config value, llama.cpp is right. If you are reproducing a paper or building custom sampling logic, the MLX approach is much cleaner. I have used both modes in different projects, and neither is wrong. They serve different audiences.

## What is genuinely missing

results.title: Whats up with MLX?
results.content: - From my benchmarks on M4 Pro 64GB with Qwen3.5 35B A3B, MLX still has a real performance edge for generation on short context: ~80 tok/s (LM Studio MLX) vs ~30 tok/s (Ollama GGUF). But MLX falls apart on large contexts. Prefill TTFT on context fills: ~14s for MLX vs ~4s for GGUF - that's 3x slower. And MLX token generation degrades as context grows, while llama.cpp stays stable. So the raw engine performance is still there for MLX, but I agree with the general sentiment: the ecosystem around GGUF (quant quality, community maintenance, template fixes) is way ahead. For daily coding work with large contexts, I'd recommend switching to GGUF.
